summary: I really honored the Merchant Marine during my time in service. Although we were separate units—the Navy and the Merchant Marine—we worked together closely. I remember a radioman who was a civilian but considered an officer in the Merchant Marine; he collected a large sum of money while I only got a couple hundred dollars after months at sea. It showed me the difference in pay and roles between the Merchant Marine and the Navy.

I served in the Navy during World War II, stationed on ships that guarded merchant vessels on long hauls across the Pacific and other regions. The Navy's role was more about service than earning money, which contrasted with the Merchant Marine. We had to carry pay cards to receive our monthly pay when in port, and life at sea was often slow and monotonous, so we kept busy playing cards and learning skills like mathematics. I also recall the Navy radioman who became a good friend and attended my wedding.

When we docked in ports like Peru, we tried to make the most of our time ashore. I remember going to bullfights there and enjoying the local culture. These shore leaves were a welcome break from the monotony of life at sea, where there was little to do but gamble and play cards. Peru was one of the places where we could spend the money we earned and experience something different during the war.

During my time in the Navy, when we were in port in places like New York City, we took advantage of the chance to see plays and movies. It was a way to enjoy some normalcy and entertainment amidst the war. These visits to big cities like New York were highlights compared to the long stretches at sea.

I remember the film Mister Roberts from the war period; it really captured the feeling of Navy life on those long hauls between islands. The Navy was like the Armed Guard, guarding ships on these extended trips, and the boredom was intense. The film showed how guys would go nuts without action, which was exactly how we felt sometimes, so we played cards and other games to keep our sanity.

I spent some time going into Africa during my Navy service. While on the bridge, which was my post, I would play cribbage and chess with the Navy radioman as we kept watch. It was a unique experience combining duty with passing time, and Africa was one of the places we visited during the war.

I had a Polish lieutenant junior grade who was very helpful in teaching me mathematics while I was in the Navy. Also, my first ship captain was Polish and a nice guy. I remember joking about how Polish people seemed to be everywhere, but these individuals made a positive impact on my service experience.

I was born and lived in Battle Creek, Michigan, which was a fairly big place. However, I was raised mostly in Marshall, about ten miles away, with my grandmother after my parents divorced. Battle Creek was my hometown before moving to Montague with my mother and stepfather.

I grew up in Marshall, Michigan, living with my grandmother after my parents divorced. It was a small town about ten miles from Battle Creek, where I was born. My upbringing there was part of my early life before moving to Montague when my mother remarried.

After my mother remarried, I moved to Montague, Michigan, a small town of about 2,000 people near White Lake. My stepfather was the superintendent of the Montague Casting Company, a gray iron foundry that employed 150 to 200 people. Life there was quiet, with no bowling alleys and just a few gas stations and saloons, so we often drove to nearby Muskegon for entertainment.

My stepfather was the superintendent of the Montague Casting Company, a gray iron foundry in Montague, Michigan. At the time, it was a decent-sized operation employing around 150 to 200 people. This job was important to my family, and when he was transferred to Tecumseh, it affected where I lived and went to school.

My stepfather was transferred to Tecumseh, Michigan, near Adrian, which was a bigger place than Montague. This transfer happened around December or January, and it meant I would have to start school over again midyear, which I wanted to avoid. So, I decided to enlist in the Navy instead of moving to Tecumseh.

Adrian, Michigan, was near Tecumseh, where my stepfather was transferred. While I didn't live in Adrian, it was part of the region connected to my family's moves during that time. The transfer to Tecumseh near Adrian influenced my decision to enlist rather than relocate and start school over.

White Lake, Michigan, was near Montague and Whitehall, and the area was known for its resort communities. Montague was a small town on the lake, and across from it was Whitehall, which had about 3,200 people and was more of a resort area. This setting was part of my life before I enlisted in the Navy.

Whitehall, Michigan, was a resort town across White Lake from Montague, where I lived. It had a population of about 3,200 people and was quite different from the industrial foundry town of Montague. We often went to nearby Muskegon for entertainment since Montague had limited options.

I vividly remember hearing about the December 7 attacks on the radio while driving home to Montague. It was a shocking moment, and the broadcaster was very specific about the news. This event happened just after Germany had sunk the battleship Hood, and it stirred up a lot of strong emotions among people, including those of German descent in the area.

Shortly before the December 7 attacks, there was the sinking of the battleship Hood by German forces in the Battle of the Denmark Strait. This event caused a lot of hard feelings and tension, especially in communities with German residents. It was part of the backdrop to my decision to enlist and the wartime atmosphere I experienced.

The HMS Hood was a British battleship sunk by German forces in the Battle of the Denmark Strait. This loss was deeply felt and caused strong emotions among people, including those in my hometown area. The sinking was part of the wartime events that influenced my perspective and the atmosphere leading up to my enlistment.
